text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION:
A 62-year-old female with a 25 pack-year former smoking history presenting with progressive cough and mild dyspnea on exertion. Chest radiograph obtained by the referring provider demonstrated a left upper lobe opacity. CT performed for further characterization and staging.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed on a 64-slice scanner following the administration of 100 mL of nonionic iodinated intravenous contrast (Omnipaque 350) with arterial and portal venous phase acquisitions. Images were reconstructed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes at 1.25 mm slice thickness with soft tissue, lung, and bone windowing algorithms. Comparison: No prior cross-sectional imaging available.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lung Parenchyma:
There is a dominant, irregularly marginated, spiculated mass in the apicoposterior segment of the left upper lobe measuring 4.2 cm in its greatest dimension (series 4, image 112). The mass demonstrates heterogeneous soft tissue attenuation with associated pleural tethering along the posterior costal pleural surface. There is a distinct radiating pattern of spicular projections extending into the surrounding parenchyma, producing a characteristic corona radiata appearance. No internal low-attenuation necrosis or cavitation is identified. The mass abuts but does not clearly invade the posterior chest wall; the extrapleural fat plane is maintained. No satellite pulmonary nodules in the left upper lobe are identified.

The right lung demonstrates no focal mass, consolidation, or dominant nodule. Scattered punctate sub-5 mm foci of increased attenuation are present in the right middle lobe and right lower lobe, most consistent with prior granulomatous disease or incidental subcentimeter pulmonary nodules; these are felt to be of indeterminate clinical significance and not suspicious for metastatic involvement given their morphology and distribution. Mild centrilobular emphysematous changes are noted bilaterally, consistent with the patient's smoking history.

Pleurae:
No pleural effusion is identified bilaterally. No pneumothorax. The pleural surfaces are otherwise smooth and unremarkable.

Mediastinum and Hila:
The mediastinum is widened. There are multiple enlarged lymph nodes in the following stations:
- Station 4L (lower left paratracheal): A cluster of enlarged lymph nodes is identified, the dominant node measuring approximately 1.8 cm in short-axis dimension, with mild heterogeneous enhancement. The lymph nodes are discrete without frank extracapsular extension.
- Station 5 (subaortic/aortopulmonary window): At least two confluent lymph nodes measuring up to 1.4 cm in short axis. These are interposed between the aortic arch and the left pulmonary artery and produce a rounded convex contour in the aortopulmonary window.
- Station 6 (para-aortic/anterior mediastinal): Lymph nodes measuring up to 1.2 cm in short axis are noted anterior to the ascending aorta and along the left side of the aortic arch.
- The subcarinal space (station 7) demonstrates no convincingly enlarged lymph nodes, though there is subtle soft tissue fullness; no dominant node exceeds 1.0 cm in short axis at this location.

No right-sided paratracheal or hilar lymphadenopathy is identified. The right hilum appears unremarkable.

Left Supraclavicular Region:
On the lower cervical/thoracic inlet images, there is a cluster of enlarged lymph nodes in the left supraclavicular fossa measuring up to 1.6 cm in short-axis dimension, with a rounded, partially necrotic morphology and peripheral enhancement. These findings are strongly suspicious for nodal metastatic involvement.

Cardiovascular:
The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The ascending and descending thoracic aorta demonstrate normal caliber without aneurysmal dilatation. Mild aortic arch calcification is noted, consistent with atherosclerotic disease. The pulmonary arteries are normal in caliber; no central filling defect to suggest pulmonary embolism.

Chest Wall and Bony Thorax:
No aggressive osseous lesion is identified in the ribs, sternum, or thoracic vertebrae. No lytic or sclerotic lesions suspicious for metastatic involvement. The posterior costal pleural interface adjacent to the left upper lobe mass is intact; no rib erosion or periosteal reaction.

Thyroid:
The thyroid gland is not fully evaluated on this study. No large dominant nodule is appreciated on the lower neck images.

ABDOMEN:

Liver:
The liver is normal in size with smooth margins and homogeneous parenchymal enhancement in both arterial and portal venous phases. No focal hepatic lesion, mass, or low-attenuation deposit is identified. No biliary ductal dilatation. The gallbladder is present without evidence of cholecystitis. Mild periportal lymph node prominence; no individual node exceeds 1.0 cm.

Spleen:
Normal in size and attenuation. No focal splenic lesion identified.

Pancreas:
The pancreas enhances homogeneously and is normal in caliber throughout the head, body, and tail. No peripancreatic stranding or ductal dilatation.

Adrenal Glands:
Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in morphology. The right adrenal gland demonstrates a normal inverted-V configuration. The left adrenal gland is unremarkable. No discrete adrenal mass or nodule is identified. No thickening or nodularity.

Kidneys:
Both kidneys are normal in size, position, and attenuation. Normal cortical enhancement is present without focal mass or hydronephrosis. Bilateral renal cortical cysts are noted, the largest measuring approximately 1.1 cm in the right kidney lower pole, with simple fluid attenuation and thin, imperceptible walls — benign by Bosniak criteria. No perirenal fluid.

Gastrointestinal Tract:
The stomach, proximal small bowel, and visualized large bowel demonstrate normal wall thickness and caliber. No bowel obstruction. The appendix is not clearly visualized but there is no surrounding inflammatory change.

Retroperitoneum and Mesentery:
No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy. The aorta is normal in caliber throughout the abdomen with no aneurysmal change. Mild infrarenal aortic calcification noted.

PELVIS:

Gynecologic Organs:
The uterus is normal in size and configuration for a postmenopausal patient. No uterine mass. The ovaries are not discretely identified, consistent with postmenopausal atrophy.

Bladder:
The urinary bladder is normally distended and demonstrates a smooth thin wall. No intraluminal filling defect.

Pelvic Lymph Nodes:
No enlarged inguinal, iliac, or obturator lymph nodes are identified.

Bony Pelvis:
No lytic or sclerotic lesion suspicious for metastatic involvement in the sacrum, iliac wings, acetabula, or proximal femora.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Dominant spiculated left upper lobe mass measuring 4.2 cm in greatest dimension with a classic corona radiata appearance, highly suspicious for a primary pulmonary malignancy. Tissue sampling is strongly recommended for definitive characterization.

2. Extensive left-sided mediastinal lymphadenopathy involving stations 4L, 5, and 6, with the dominant nodes measuring up to 1.8 cm in short axis. The distribution and morphology raise strong concern for locoregional nodal metastatic involvement from the left upper lobe primary.

3. Left supraclavicular lymphadenopathy with partially necrotic morphology, measuring up to 1.6 cm, highly suspicious for metastatic nodal involvement. In conjunction with the mediastinal findings, this raises concern for N3 nodal disease.

4. No pulmonary nodules in the contralateral lung with features convincingly suspicious for metastatic involvement. Scattered bilateral sub-5 mm nodules noted, likely representing inflammatory or granulomatous foci.

5. No evidence of hepatic, adrenal, osseous, or intraabdominal metastatic disease on this study. The bilateral adrenal glands are normal in appearance.

6. No pleural effusion or pericardial effusion.

7. Background centrilobular emphysema, consistent with prior smoking history.

Correlation with functional imaging (PET-CT) and tissue sampling is recommended. Findings were communicated to the referring clinician at the time of final report.

Signing Radiologist: [Attending Radiologist, Body Imaging / Thoracic Radiology]
Reviewed and electronically signed.
